article_title: An ultrasensitive method for detection of cell-free RNA
abstract: Sensitive methods for detection of cell-free RNA (cfRNA) could facilitate non-invasive gene expression profiling and monitoring of diseases (Ref: 1-6). Here we describe RARE-seq (random priming and affinity capture of cfRNA fragments for enrichment analysis by sequencing), a method optimized for cfRNA analysis. We demonstrate that platelet contamination can substantially confound cfRNA analyses and develop an approach to overcome it. In analytical validations, we find RARE-seq to be approximately 50-fold more sensitive for detecting tumour-derived cfRNA than whole-transcriptome RNA sequencing (RNA-seq), with a limit of detection of 0.05%. To explore clinical utility, we profiled 437 plasma samples from 369 individuals with cancer or non-malignant conditions and controls. Detection of non-small-cell lung cancer expression signatures in cfRNA increased with stage (6 out of 20 (30%) in stage I; 5 out of 8 (63%) in stage II; 10 out of 15 (67%) in stage III; 80 out of 96 (83% sensitivity) in stage IV at 95% specificity) and RARE-seq was more sensitive than tumour-naive circulating tumour DNA (ctDNA) analysis. In patients with EGFR-mutant non-small-cell lung cancer who developed resistance to tyrosine kinase inhibitors, we detected both histological transformation and mutation-based resistance mechanisms. Finally, we demonstrate the potential utility of RARE-seq for determination of tissue of origin, assessing benign pulmonary conditions and tracking response to mRNA vaccines. These results highlight the potential value of ultrasensitive cfRNA analysis and provide proof of concept for diverse clinical applications.
introduction:
Blood-based liquid biopsies enable non-invasive characterization of diverse cancers with existing applications including early detection, tissue-of-origin (TOO) classification, genotyping and disease monitoring. These applications have largely focused on ctDNA, for example, as routinely used clinically for non-invasive tumour genotyping of actionable mutations in patients with advanced malignancies. cfRNA analysis could potentially complement ctDNA analysis and allow broader molecular characterization of cancers (Ref: 1-6). However, the vast majority of circulating transcripts are shed by haematopoietic cells (Ref: 7-10), making detection of rare circulating tumour RNA (ctRNA) molecules challenging.
Here we describe RARE-seq, a method specifically optimized for cfRNA analysis. We demonstrate that this approach improves the limit of detection for ctRNA over previous methods while maintaining high specificity for tumour-naive cancer detection. We further explore the potential utility of RARE-seq in diverse clinical applications, including non-invasive ctRNA genotyping, treatment resistance monitoring, TOO analysis and molecular characterization of non-malignant conditions.

subsections.title: Pre-analytical factors affecting cfRNA
subsections.content: As previously described (Ref: 1,8,11), we observed that cfRNA is highly fragmented and does not contain detectable 18S and 28S rRNA peaks (Extended Data Fig. 1a), indicating high levels of degradation. In blood samples from healthy donors with no history of cancer, the median concentration of cfRNA was 220 pg per millilitre plasma, representing the equivalent of approximately 20 cells (n = 117; Extended Data Fig. 1b). We therefore began by evaluating factors that affected cfRNA recovery and downstream expression analysis (Supplementary Note). We identified multiple blood collection and RNA extraction protocols that allowed similar RNA recovery from plasma (Extended Data Fig. 1c,d) and found that factors such as haemolysis were not associated with cfRNA yields (Extended Data Fig. 1e). When investigating the effects of sample storage, we observed a decrease of cfRNA concentration during the first month of freezer storage (P = 0.003; Extended Data Fig. 1f), but no significant effect after the first month for up to 10 years (P = 0.74; Extended Data Fig. 1g). We next optimized multiple steps in the assay workflow, including removal of contaminating DNA, complementary DNA (cDNA) synthesis and end repair. These optimizations enhanced library preparation efficiency from low cfRNA inputs and minimized effects of contaminating cfDNA (Extended Data Fig. 2a–h). We further confirmed that our optimizations resulted in reproducible cfRNA expression profiles in healthy controls (Extended Data Fig. 2i–k). Collectively, we refer to the optimized method as RARE-seq (Fig. 1a).

subsections.title: Platelet genes are enriched in cfRNA
subsections.content:
We next characterized expression differences between cellular and cfRNA by applying RARE-seq to cfRNA and matched leukocyte RNA from ten healthy adults. Differential expression analysis identified 8,336 significant genes, including 5,271 genes over-represented in cfRNA and 3,065 genes over-represented in leukocyte RNA (Fig. 1b). Using gene set enrichment analysis (GSEA) and cell-type-specific gene sets from a single-cell RNA-seq atlas (Ref: 12) (Fig. 1c), we found that cfRNA was significantly enriched for non-haematopoietic cell types, including endothelial cells, hepatocytes and fibroblasts (n = 71 total), consistent with previous work (Ref: 10). By contrast, leukocyte RNA was significantly enriched for 12 major haematopoietic cell types of myeloid and lymphoid lineages, including T cells, natural killer cells, neutrophils and monocytes. Only two haematopoietic cell types were enriched in cfRNA — erythroid precursors and platelets — suggesting their contributions to the cfRNA pool during erythropoiesis and megakaryocytopoiesis.
Given the significant enrichment of platelet transcripts in cfRNA (Fig. 1c), we suspected that residual platelets not removed during plasma isolation may confound cfRNA analysis. To test this, we compared cfRNA concentrations using three different centrifugation speeds to isolate plasma (n = 32). Consistent with our hypothesis, the median concentration was approximately 7.5-fold higher at 1,200g than at 2,500g (1.72 ng ml−1 versus 0.23 ng ml−1; P = 0.000052; Fig. 1d). Furthermore, average expression of platelet-specific genes (n = 130) (Ref: 12) was significantly associated with centrifugation speed (Fig. 1e). Principal component analysis revealed that the first principal component (PC1) captured 70% of expression variation and was strongly associated with platelet expression (R = −0.99, P ≤ 2.2 × 10−16; Fig. 1f,g). Similar results were found even among samples processed with a fixed centrifugation speed, suggesting that differences in platelet counts may also contribute to variation of platelet transcripts in cfRNA (R = 0.98, P ≤ 1.3 × 10−11; Fig. 1h). Collectively, these results suggest that platelets represent the largest source of expression variation in cfRNA and that platelet contamination is a critical pre-analytical variable to control for in cfRNA analyses.
Although individual platelets contain less RNA content than leukocytes (Ref: 13), platelets outnumber leukocytes by approximately two orders of magnitude in blood. In addition, physiological variation in platelet counts span an approximately threefold range in healthy adults (Ref: 14) with further variability observed in the context of cancer (Ref: 15). Therefore, even optimized methods to minimize plasma contamination by platelets could fail to completely remove platelet-derived RNA from cfRNA samples. Separately, from a practical standpoint, it is often not feasible to standardize blood banking protocols, especially when profiling previously collected samples. Therefore, we investigated whether expression contributions from platelet contamination could be algorithmically eliminated. To do so, we developed an in silico approach to remove unwanted gene expression driven by varying platelet contamination levels (Fig. 1i). This approach aims to separate wanted variation (that is, cancer-associated gene expression differences) from unwanted variation (that is, technical or biological noise) by performing factor analysis of expression from negative control genes for which only unwanted variation is expected to be present (Methods). We found that this approach successfully removed the unwanted variation contributed by platelets, as shown by the lack of the correlation between platelet expression and PC1 after correction (R = 0.07, P = 0.69; Fig. 1j). Hierarchical clustering further confirmed this effect, as samples clustered according to platelet gene expression before correction (Fig. 1k), but according to inter-individual expression differences after correction (Fig. 1l). Therefore, addressing platelet contamination in cfRNA analyses is critical for reliably uncovering gene expression contributions from non-platelet sources.
figures: Fig. 1 | Platelet and non-haematopoietic transcripts are enriched in cfRNA. a, Schematic of the RARE-seq method. The schematic was adapted from ref. (Ref: 36), Springer Nature. b, Differential expression analysis comparing cfRNA and matched leukocyte RNA from healthy donors (n = 10 pairs). Significance was determined using Wald test and adjusted using Benjamini–Hochberg correction. c, GSEA using cell-type-specific signatures from PanglaoDB (Ref: 12) (n = 170 cell types). The top 15 positively and negatively enriched cell types are shown, and the dashed line delineates significant enrichment. GI, gastrointestinal. d, Relationship between centrifugation speed during plasma isolation and cfRNA concentration per millilitre plasma (n = 10 at 1,200g, n = 6 at 1,800g and n = 16 at 2,500g). Comparisons were performed using the Kruskal–Wallis test. e, Relationship between centrifugation speed and platelet-specific gene expression in cfRNA samples from panel d. Comparisons were performed using the Kruskal–Wallis test. Average log2NX, mean of normalized expression. f, Principal component analysis of cfRNA expression clusters according to spin speed (PC1) and sex (PC2). The top 1,000 variable genes in cfRNA samples from panel d were used for principal component analysis. g, Association between average platelet-specific gene expression and PC1 in cfRNA samples from panel d before correction. Pearson and Spearman correlation are shown. h, Association between average platelet-specific gene expression and PC1 when considering only samples processed using the same centrifugation speed (n = 16 at 2,500g) before correction. i, Schematic of the platelet-directed correction approach (Methods). SVD, singular value decomposition; W, platelet correction factor; Z, z scores; Zp, z scores of platelet genes. j, Association between platelet-specific gene expression and PC1 after platelet-directed correction. k, Hierarchical clustering of the top 1,000 variable genes in cfRNA before correction. Clustering was performed using Euclidean distance. l, Hierarchical clustering of the top 1,000 variable genes after platelet-directed correction. In the boxplots, the centre line denotes the median, the box contains the interquartile range (IQR), and the whiskers denote extrema no farther than 1.5× IQR.

subsections.title: Enhancing sensitivity of ctRNA detection
subsections.content:
Although cfRNA is highly enriched for expression of genes from non-haematopoietic sources, the absolute expression levels of these genes are relatively low compared with haematopoietic-derived transcripts (for example, globins) and highly expressed housekeeping genes (for example, β-actin). We therefore hypothesized that selectively capturing genes absent from or lowly expressed in healthy control cfRNA could enhance detection of non-haematopoietic tissue or disease signatures and expand the utility of RARE-seq to a wide range of clinical applications. To test this, we identified such ‘rare abundance genes’ (RAGs) using whole-coding transcriptome RARE-seq data from healthy cfRNA samples (n = 50) and RNA-seq data from healthy whole-blood samples (n = 307) from the GTEx project (Ref: 16) (Fig. 2a). We defined RAGs based on consistent low or absent expression in cfRNA and whole blood (n = 5,632 genes; Extended Data Fig. 3a and Supplementary Table 1). As compared with all protein-coding transcripts, RAGs were substantially enriched for both non-haematopoietic tissue-specific genes and cancer-specific genes from diverse cancers (Fig. 2b,c).
The enrichment of genes specific for multiple tissue and cancer types among RAGs suggests that a RAG-targeted sequencing panel could increase sensitivity for detection of cancer or other conditions of interest without requiring a bespoke panel design for each application. We therefore designed a capture panel targeting 4,737 RAGs and 50 housekeeping genes, prioritizing genes with relatively uniform expression across healthy cfRNA and whole-blood samples while covering a broad dynamic range of expression (Extended Data Fig. 3b). Although these modules comprise the core of the RAG-focused capture panel design and could be used by themselves to explore diverse pathophysiological conditions without further customization, additional targets may be optionally added to the capture panel if desired (Extended Data Fig. 3c). For example, for the purposes of this study, we added genes known to be recurrently mutated in lung cancers (n = 122 genes), including genes such as TP53, KRAS, and RET (Extended Data Fig. 3d; Methods). The full collection of captured genes is listed in Supplementary Table 2.
We next profiled five healthy cfRNA samples using both whole-coding transcriptome and RAG capture panels using RARE-seq. Gene expression was highly correlated between matched libraries (R = 0.98, P ≤ 2.2 × 10−16; Extended Data Fig. 3e), including for the housekeeping genes (R = 0.97, P ≤ 2.2 × 10−16; Extended Data Fig. 3f). Within the subset of genes shared across both capture panels, RAG capture resulted in higher unique sequencing depth (Fig. 2d), more genes detected (Fig. 2e) and higher unique sequencing depth per gene detected (Fig. 2f). Thus, targeted capture of RAGs effectively increases sensitivity for detection of genes that are lowly expressed in healthy cfRNA.
We next set out to determine the limit of 95% detection (LOD95) for transcriptional signatures of interest, using either whole-coding transcriptome or RAG capture panels. To score the presence of a gene signature of interest in cfRNA samples, we developed an analytical framework to compute a cfRNA gene signature enrichment score by comparing the expression of pre-defined signature genes in a sample of interest to a ‘meta-reference’ consisting of cfRNA samples from healthy controls and using bootstrapping to estimate its significance (Fig. 2g; Methods). An important advantage of this approach is that unlike machine learning-based methods, it does not require a large training cohort of patient cfRNA samples, as it leverages existing gene expression datasets to define gene signatures and associated gene-wise weights based on their expression levels in the tissue or condition of interest.
To establish the LOD95 of RARE-seq, we generated in silico serial dilutions of RNA from the NCI-H1975 NSCLC cell line into healthy control cfRNA at various cancer fractions. To enable using our cfRNA enrichment score framework, we generated an NCI-H1975-specific gene signature, comprising the top differentially expressed genes (DEGs) distinguishing NCI-H1975 from a meta-reference healthy cfRNA profile (H1975 Sig; n = 122 genes). Using this signature, the LOD95 for RAG RARE-seq was 0.05% (Fig. 2h), more than 50-fold more sensitive than for whole-coding transcriptome RARE-seq (2.8%; Fig. 2i), demonstrating the value of targeting RAGs. To experimentally confirm this result, we created in vitro dilution series, by spiking NCI-H1975 RNA into healthy donor cfRNA at defined concentrations. These in vitro spikes demonstrated strong agreement with our in silico results (Fig. 2h,i). We also evaluated the effect of sequencing coverage depth (range of 10–50 million reads) on the LOD95 of RAG-targeted RARE-seq and found that higher sequencing depth improved the LOD95 (Extended Data Fig. 3i). Accordingly, we targeted 50 million reads for subsequent experiments using RARE-seq with the RAG panel.
figures: Fig. 2 | Targeting transcripts absent from healthy cfRNA improves analytical sensitivity for lung cancer detection. a, Schematic depicting the selection of RAGs in plasma. The illustrations of the plasma and blood tubes were created using BioRender (https://biorender.com). b,c, Fraction of tissue-enriched (b) and cancer-enriched (c) genes that overlap RAGs. Tissue-enriched and cancer-enriched genes were selected as defined in the Methods. Comparisons were performed using two-sided Fisher’s exact test and adjusted with Benjamini–Hochberg correction (**P < 0.01, ****P < 0.0001). NS, not significant. d, Unique depth in libraries captured using either whole-coding transcriptome or RAG capture panels (n = 5 matched libraries). Libraries were downsampled to have equivalent sequencing depth and filtered to include regions captured by both panels. Comparisons were performed using paired two-sided Student’s t-test (***P = 0.0009). e, Number of shared genes detected in libraries in panel d. The threshold for detection was one or more unique reads. Comparisons were performed using paired two-sided Student’s t-test (***P = 0.0008). f, Histogram depicting the difference in unique depth per gene for libraries in panel d. The dashed line depicts equivalent depth between capture panels. g, Schematic of cfRNA enrichment score (ES) analytical framework (see Methods). The data matrices, scatter plot and arrows were created using BioRender (https://biorender.com). h, Relationship between cfRNA enrichment score detection rate and lung cancer cell line (NCI-H1975) fraction for spikes captured with the RAG panel (n = 3 replicates per spike). The presence of cancer RNA was detected in each spike using an NCI-H1975 cfRNA enrichment score. Logistic regression was used to estimate the LOD95 (shown as dashed line) from in silico spikes (shown as grey circles). Results from in vitro spikes are shown as red triangles. i, Empirical LOD95 of NCI-H1975 spikes captured using the whole-coding transcriptome panel (n = 1 replicate per spike) evaluated using the same approach as panel h. In the boxplots, the centre line denotes the median, the box contains the IQR, and the whiskers denote extrema no farther than 1.5× IQR.

subsections.title: Detection of non-small-cell lung cancer
subsections.content:
Having developed and optimized RARE-seq, we focused the remainder of our study on generating proof-of-concept data for cfRNA analysis across several clinical applications. Given the higher analytic sensitivity observed by capturing RAGs, we used RAG-focused RARE-seq for these experiments. First, we investigated performance for tumour-naive detection of lung adenocarcinomas (LUADs), the most common type of non-small-cell lung cancer (NSCLC). We analysed cfRNA from 139 patients with LUAD (n = 20 patients with stage I, n = 8 patients with stage II, n = 15 patients with stage III and n = 96 patients with stage IV) and 50 controls without cancer, including 26 risk-matched controls with a history of substantial tobacco exposure collected at the time of low-dose computed tomography (LDCT) screening for lung cancer. Concentrations of cfRNA and sequencing depth were similar between groups (Extended Data Fig. 4a,b). Platelet-adjusted differential expression analysis between LUADs and controls identified 96 genes overexpressed in LUADs, including key genes known to be highly expressed in LUADs such as SLC34A2, SFTA2 and NKX2-1 (also known as TTF1; Fig. 3a). LUAD DEGs were confirmed to be highly expressed in LUAD tumours from The Cancer Genome Atlas (TCGA) (Ref: 17), whereas were depleted in meta-reference cfRNA controls (Fig. 3b). In addition, using cell-type-specific gene signatures, GSEA revealed LUAD cfRNA to be most enriched for epithelial cells including pulmonary alveolar type II cells, and most depleted for naive and memory B lymphocytes (Fig. 3c). Thus, cfRNA from patients with LUAD is enriched for transcripts that are highly expressed in LUAD tumours.
To further quantify the enrichment of LUAD-derived transcripts, we applied the cfRNA enrichment score framework developed above using a LUAD-specific gene signature (LUAD Sig; n = 72 genes) identified using RNA-seq data from LUAD tumours within TCGA (Ref: 17), LUAD cell lines from the Cancer Cell Line Encyclopedia (CCLE) (Ref: 18), whole blood (GTEx (Ref: 16)) and meta-reference cfRNA controls (Methods). The LUAD Sig was detected in 101 out of 139 participants with LUAD (73% sensitivity at 95% specificity in non-cancer controls; Fig. 3d and Supplementary Table 3) and was significantly associated with stage (for sensitivity by stage, I = 30%, II = 62%, III = 67% and IV = 83%; P = 9.1 × 10−16; Fig. 3e). In addition, in a subset of plasma samples that were also interrogated using tumour-naive ctDNA analysis (n = 38; Methods), the LUAD cfRNA enrichment score was significantly correlated with ctDNA variant allele frequency (R = 0.43, P = 0.009; Fig. 3f). Of these samples, 34% was detected by only RARE-seq and none was detected by only ctDNA analysis (Fig. 3g), indicating that tumour-naive ctRNA analysis is more sensitive than tumour-naive ctDNA analysis. Technical variables that varied across groups, such as library input and unique sequencing depth, extraction method and capture panel, were not associated with sensitivity (Extended Data Fig. 4e–h), indicating that these variables did not drive detection. In addition, detection was not significantly associated with biological factors such as patient smoking status or the presence of oncogenic driver mutations (Extended Data Fig. 4i–k). Finally, we found that platelet correction significantly improved sensitivity (P = 0.03; Extended Data Fig. 4l).

subsections.title: Genotyping of somatic mutations in ctRNA
subsections.content:
We also explored the ability to genotype cancer-derived somatic mutations using RARE-seq. Although non-invasive tumour genotyping is already routinely performed clinically using ctDNA and ctRNA-based genotyping is unlikely to outperform it, variant detection could be a useful addition to cfRNA analysis. We therefore developed a custom ctRNA genotyping approach to detect clinically actionable variants, including single-nucleotide variants (SNVs), insertions or deletions (indels), splice variants and gene fusions, in recurrent NSCLC oncogenes. To ensure specificity, putative variants were censored if their variant allele frequency did not significantly exceed the background error rate observed in a subset of withheld controls (n = 50; Extended Data Fig. 5a; Methods). Performance was assessed in 136 patients with stage IV LUAD with known variants from previous tumour or ctDNA genotyping, as well as 97 healthy and LDCT controls (Supplementary Table 4). We detected one or more somatic driver mutations in 28% of patients with LUAD and 1% of controls, including key NSCLC oncogenic drivers such as L858R and exon 19 deletions in EGFR, G12C in KRAS, ROS1 and RET fusions, and MET exon skipping events (Fig. 3h,i and Extended Data Fig. 5b–d). Of the 162 variants identified by tumour tissue genomic DNA or ctDNA genotyping in the same patients, 35 (22%) were also detectable in ctRNA, and detection rates were similar across variant types (Fig. 3j). Of the 34 variants identified by tumour tissue genomic DNA in patients with matched cfRNA and cfDNA data available, 11 variants were detectable in ctDNA compared with 3 variants detectable in ctRNA, indicating that ctRNA genotyping may be useful to supplement expression-based analyses but should not replace ctDNA genotyping (Extended Data Fig. 5e). We observed a significant relationship between variant detection rates and corresponding expression levels; for instance, EGFR variants were more frequently detected in cfRNA samples with high EGFR expression (P = 0.0001; Fig. 3k). Thus, RARE-seq analysis allows simultaneous identification of somatic mutations in patients with cancer.
Given that ctRNA expression signatures and ctRNA somatic variation could individually distinguish between patients with cancer and controls without cancer, we next investigated whether a machine learning approach could combine these features into a single detection algorithm. We trained a weighted elastic net classifier to distinguish LUAD and control cfRNA using tenfold nested cross-validation in a training cohort of 96 stage IV cases and 47 controls. The model demonstrated high classification performance (area under receiver operating characteristic curve, AUROC = 0.94; Fig. 3l,m and Supplementary Table 5) and selected 23 features including gene expression features (for example, key LUAD markers such as SLC34A2) and mutation-based features (that is, presence of L858R in EGFR or a KRAS mutation; Extended Data Fig. 5f). To test whether our cohort was sufficiently large, we repeated classifier training and cross-validation using sub-sampling and found stable performance when using at least 100 samples for model training (Extended Data Fig. 5g). The elastic net model performed similarly well in a withheld validation cohort collected at an independent institution (AUROC = 0.95; Fig. 3l,m). Performance of the elastic net classifier was statistically similar to the enrichment score framework in the training cohort (P = 0.27), but improved performance in the validation cohort (P = 0.001). Therefore, when sufficient samples are available, machine learning-based approaches can also be used to train robust classifiers for RARE-seq.
figures: Fig. 3 | Detection of ctRNA in plasma from patients with NSCLC. a, Differential expression between LUAD cfRNA (n = 139) and non-cancer cfRNA (n = 50). Significance was determined using Wald test and Benjamini–Hochberg correction. b, Average expression of genes enriched in LUAD cfRNA in LUAD tumours (n = 385) and meta-reference cfRNA (n = 15). Comparison used two-sided Wilcoxon test (****P ≤ 2.2 × 10−16). c, GSEA using PanglaoDB (Ref: 12) cell-type signatures. Top 15 positive and negative types are shown, and the dashed line delineates significant enrichment. d, Enrichment scores for the LUAD Sig (n = 72 genes) in cfRNA from patients with LUAD (n = 139), risk-matched controls (n = 26) and healthy controls (n = 24). e, Sensitivity of LUAD cfRNA enrichment score detection, summarized by stage. Detection threshold achieving 95% or more specificity was used. Error bars represent the 95% binomial confidence interval. Stages were compared using Spearman correlation. f, Relationship between LUAD cfRNA enrichment score and ctDNA allele frequency (AF) in matched plasma (n = 38). ND, not detected. g, Percentage of matched samples with detectable ctRNA and/or ctDNA. h, Oncoprint of variants detected in cfRNA from patients with stage IV LUAD with one or more tissue-adjudicated or ctDNA-adjudicated variants and controls. i, Percentage of samples with one or more variants detected in cfRNA. j, Percentage of known variants detected in cfRNA. k, Relationship between EGFR expression and variant detection for samples with known EGFR variants. l, Probability of lung cancer from the elastic net (EN) model (for training, n = 96 LUAD and 47 control; for validation, n = 52 LUAD and 46 control). Detection threshold achieving 95% or more specificity in the training cohort was used (shown as dashed line). m, Receiver operating characteristic curves summarizing detection using LUAD cfRNA enrichment score and elastic net probabilities. Area under the curve (AUC) was compared with DeLong’s test. CV, cross-validation. In the boxplots, the centre line denotes the median, the box contains the IQR, and the whiskers denote extrema no farther than 1.5× IQR.

subsections.title: Identifying resistance to EGFR therapies
subsections.content:
One of our motivations for developing RARE-seq was the hypothesis that in addition to identifying emergent somatic mutations mediating resistance, ctRNA analysis might allow detection of non-genetic therapeutic resistance mechanisms, such as histological transformation (Ref: 19). To explore this question, we focused on patients with EGFR-mutant LUAD treated with EGFR tyrosine kinase inhibitors (TKIs). Acquired resistance to EGFR TKIs is caused by heterogenous mechanisms, including both EGFR-dependent mechanisms (such as secondary point mutations in the EGFR gene) and EGFR-independent mechanisms (such as bypass pathway activation or histological transformation) (Ref: 20,21). We profiled plasma samples from 10 patients with stage IV EGFR-mutant LUAD who had received osimertinib after progressing on at least one previous EGFR TKI and who then developed resistance to osimertinib. The mechanism of resistance was determined by tissue biopsy at the time of progression, revealing small-cell histological transformation in four patients, MET gene amplifications in three patients, and emergent C797S point mutations in EGFR in another three patients (Fig. 4a). For each patient, blood was collected before the start of osimertinib and at one or more timepoints after progression (n = 24 timepoints total).
Histological transformation to small-cell lung cancer (SCLC) was evaluated using a SCLC signature (SCLC Sig) defined using public tumour gene expression data (Ref: 18,22). The signature comprised 73 genes, including canonical SCLC markers such as ASCL1, NEUROD1 and INSM1. At time of radiological progression, the SCLC Sig was detected in three of four (75%) patients with biopsy-proven histological transformation (Fig. 4b). For one of these patients, we also analysed a plasma sample collected 274 days after the initiation of SCLC-targeted chemotherapy (for example, carboplatin/etoposide) and observed decreased SCLC cfRNA enrichment score, suggesting treatment response of the small cell component (Fig. 4c).
We next assessed the performance of RARE-seq for identifying genetic mechanisms of resistance. To detect MET pathway activation caused by MET amplification using ctRNA, we developed a MET amplification signature (METamp Sig) containing nine genes differentially expressed in three EGFR-mutant NSCLC cell lines with acquired resistance due to MET amplification (Methods). The METamp Sig was detected in two of three (67%) patients with biopsy-proven MET amplification (Fig. 4d). Of note, we observed a decreased METamp cfRNA enrichment score in one patient after the MET inhibitor savolitinib was added to osimertinib, coincident with a partial response by imaging (Fig. 4e). We then identified EGFRC797S-mutant reads in two of three (67%) patients known to have acquired this mutation based on tumour DNA or ctDNA analysis (Fig. 4f,g). These resistance mechanisms were not detected in any pre-resistance timepoint, confirming the specificity of our approach (Supplementary Table 6). Together, these results demonstrate proof of concept that RARE-seq can identify both non-genetic and genetic mechanisms underlying EGFR TKI resistance, including histological transformation, which is not detectable by mutation-based ctDNA approaches.
figures: Fig. 4 | Detecting mechanisms of resistance to EGFR TKIs in cfRNA. a, Summary of the EGFR TKI cohort (n = 10 patients, n = 24 timepoints). Mechanisms of resistance were determined via tissue biopsy. C797S, EGFRC797S SNV; METamp, MET amplification; tSCLC, histological transformation to SCLC. The illustrations of the plasma tubes and tissue block were created using BioRender (https://biorender.com). b, Enrichment scores for SCLC Sig (n = 73 genes) in patients with biopsy-proven tSCLC. The threshold representing 95% or more specificity in controls is shown by the dashed line. c, Vignette of a patient with tSCLC after EGFR TKI treatment. The left axis depicts the enrichment or elastic net scores, and the right axis depicts the allele frequency (AF) of tumour-adjudicated variants detected in cfRNA. PD, progressive disease; PR, partial response; SD, stable disease. d, Enrichment score for a METamp Sig (n = 9 genes) in patients with biopsy-proven MET amplification. The threshold representing 95% or more specificity in controls is shown by the dashed line. e, Vignette of a patient with emergence of MET amplification after EGFR TKI treatment. f, EGFRC797S allele frequency in cfRNA from patients with biopsy-proven C797S mutation. g, Vignette of a patient with emergence of a EGFRC797S mutation after EGFR TKI treatment. In the boxplots, the centre line denotes the median, the box contains the IQR, and the whiskers denote extrema no farther than 1.5× IQR.

subsections.title: Tissue-of-origin determination in ctRNA
subsections.content: We also wanted to test whether the same RARE-seq assay could be used to detect other cancer types. We therefore analysed plasma samples from patients with three types of other carcinomas: pancreatic adenocarcinoma (PAAD; n = 10 patients with stage IV), prostate adenocarcinoma (PRAD; n = 1 patient with stage III and n = 8 patients with stage IV) and liver hepatocellular carcinoma (LIHC; n = 3 patients with stage I, n = 5 patients with stage II and n = 2 patients with stage IV). Cancer-specific gene signatures were generated for each cancer type and evaluated using the cfRNA enrichment score framework (Supplementary Table 7). Sensitivity of cancer-specific cfRNA enrichment score detection was 80%, 90% and 100% for LIHC, PAAD and PRAD, respectively, after adjusting for multiple hypothesis testing (Fig. 5a). Given that cancer-specific signatures were detectable in multiple cancer types, we next wanted to explore whether ctRNA analysis can distinguish between cancer types. The ability to identify the TOO of a malignancy non-invasively could be helpful for patients with advanced-stage cancers of unknown primary who have no clinically detected primary tumour. Thus, we generated additional cancer signatures capturing genes uniquely expressed in each cancer compared with all other cancers, as well as to cfRNA from patients without cancer (cancer-TOO signatures). TOO was assessed for any advanced-stage cancer sample detected by at least one cancer-specific signature. The top predicted TOO was correct 89% of the time and the top two predicted TOOs identified the correct tumour type 96% of the time (Fig. 5b,c). Thus, cfRNA analysis could potentially enable non-invasive TOO identification.

subsections.title: Application to non-malignant conditions
subsections.content:
We also explored several potential applications of RARE-seq beyond oncological settings, by first testing whether cfRNA expression might reveal lung injury caused by benign pulmonary conditions. We profiled cfRNA from 3 patients with chronic obstructive pulmonary disorder, 7 patients with active COVID-19 infections (n = 13 timepoints) and 19 patients admitted to the intensive care unit with evidence of pulmonary injury (including 9 patients with acute respiratory distress syndrome). Across these diverse pulmonary conditions, lung-derived cfRNA was detectable in 49% of patients using a normal lung gene expression signature derived from GTEx (Ref: 16) (Fig. 5d and Supplementary Table 8). By contrast, among adult participants without known pulmonary conditions (n = 42), lung cfRNA was detected in 14%, with detection being significantly more frequent in current smokers than in former- or never-smokers (45.5% versus 3.6%, P = 0.004; Fig. 5e). This result suggests that lung cfRNA is induced by ongoing injury to pulmonary epithelium caused by recent exposure to tobacco smoke (Ref: 23,24). Conversely, in patients with known pulmonary conditions, detection was significantly greater in those who were on a ventilator at the time of blood collection (Fig. 5f), probably reflecting the severity of the pulmonary condition as well as insult to pulmonary epithelium from mechanical ventilation (Ref: 25). Thus, cfRNA analysis may have utility for assessment of benign conditions involving acute and chronic patterns of tissue injury.
Separately, given the rapidly growing interest in RNA-based vaccines and therapeutics, we explored whether RARE-seq might have utility for simultaneously tracking RNA-based treatments and their effects on the host. To do so, we longitudinally profiled plasma from five individuals receiving mRNA-based COVID-19 vaccinations (n = 20 timepoints). We performed RARE-seq on these samples using the RAG capture panel supplemented with baits targeting the sequence of the mRNA vaccine (Extended Data Fig. 3c). Vaccine RNA was detectable immediately after vaccination and persisted for up to 6 weeks after injection (Fig. 5g). Host genes that were differentially expressed after vaccination were enriched for inflammatory responses, interferon-γ responses and cytokine signalling, reflecting immune activation in response to vaccination (Fig. 5h). Vaccine RNA and host immune response genes were both highest in the first week post-vaccination (Fig. 5i). These results highlight the potential of RARE-seq for simultaneously measuring pharmacokinetic and pharmacodynamic aspects of RNA-based therapies.
figures: Fig. 5 | Exploring additional potential applications of RARE-seq. a, Sensitivity of detection of LIHC cfRNA (n = 10), PAAD (n = 10) and PRAD (n = 9) enrichment scores using the respective cancer-specific signatures. Detection thresholds achieving 90% or more specificity after multiple hypothesis correction were used. Error bars represent the 95% binomial confidence interval. b, Accuracy of TOO determination using cancer-TOO signatures in samples detected by at least one cancer-specific signature (n = 105). The shading depicts enrichment score rank for the true cancer type. c, Confusion matrix comparing predicted and true cancer type. d, Enrichment score for normal lung tissue signature (n = 5 genes) in cfRNA from patients with benign pulmonary conditions (n = 77 timepoints from 71 individuals). ARDS, acute respiratory distress syndrome; COPD, chronic obstructive pulmonary disease; COVID, COVID-19 infection; ICU, intensive care unit. e, Relationship between normal lung enrichment score and smoking history. Samples without known pulmonary conditions were compared using two-sided Wilcoxon test (n = 42, P = 0.00095). f, Relationship between normal lung enrichment score and ventilator status. Samples with known pulmonary conditions were compared using two-sided Wilcoxon test (n = 35 timepoints from 29 individuals, P = 0.0061). g, Timecourse depicting COVID-19 mRNA vaccine expression in cfRNA (n = 20 timepoints from 5 individuals). Data are presented as mean log fold change above baseline ± s.d. h, GSEA of DEGs in post-vaccination timepoints. DEGs were determined by a likelihood ratio test, and GSEA significance was determined using fgsea (Methods). The top ten MSigDb Hallmark sets are displayed (Ref: 37-39). DN, down. i, Heatmap showing COVID-19 mRNA vaccine and DEGs in post-vaccination timepoints. Expression was centred and scaled using pre-vaccination levels. In the boxplots, the centre line denotes the median, the box contains the IQR, and the whiskers denote extrema no farther than 1.5× IQR.

discussion:
Here we describe a method for cfRNA profiling, demonstrate its performance in NSCLC and provide proof-of-concept results for various additional clinical applications. After end-to-end optimization, we found that RARE-seq achieves a limit of detection below that of tumour-naive ctDNA-based methods (Ref: 26-30), resulting in a higher sensitivity for detection of NSCLC. A key innovation of our approach is the specific capture of transcripts that are absent or very lowly expressed in plasma of healthy individuals, facilitating improved sensitivity for transcripts stemming from non-haematological tissues. These transcripts are highly enriched for cancer-specific and solid organ-specific genes and therefore useful for detection of pathophysiology occurring outside of the blood. RAG-focused RARE-seq can be applied to diverse pathophysiological conditions without the need for bespoke capture panels.
One important observation was the high proportion of platelet-derived transcripts present in cfRNA. In contrast with cfDNA shed by megakaryocytes (Ref: 31), these transcripts largely stem from intact platelets that partition with plasma during blood sample processing and represent a major confounder for cfRNA analyses. Indeed, expression differences in cfRNA of patients with cancer versus controls reported in several previous studies appear to largely stem from differences in platelet-derived transcripts (Ref: 2,32,33). We therefore developed a computational approach for eliminating confounding effects caused by platelet RNA and found that this significantly improved sensitivity for detection of lung cancer. Although platelet contamination can also be reduced using pre-analytical approaches such as increasing centrifugation speed (Ref: 34), even platelet poor plasma preparations are seldom entirely platelet free. In addition, such procedures are not feasible when analysing previously banked samples. Therefore, we anticipate that our approach for removing platelet contributions to cfRNA gene expression profiles will be broadly useful for future cfRNA studies.
Our exploration of potential applications suggests that ultrasensitive cfRNA analysis may be useful in diverse clinical settings. For example, a promising application is non-invasive identification of cancer types. Such an approach could be useful for non-invasive identification of TOO in patients with cancers of unknown primary or distinguishing between cancer subtypes. As our approach enables tumour-naive detection of ctRNA, it could potentially be used for cancer screening, either alone or in combination with other diagnostic modalities. However, future studies evaluating large clinical cohorts of patients with early-stage cancers and risk-matched controls will be required to rigorously test cfRNA for screening applications. The fact that RARE-seq detected lung cancer ctRNA in many samples that were negative by tumour-naive ctDNA analysis suggests that the two approaches may be complimentary.
When considering patients with EGFR-mutant NSCLC treated with EGFR TKIs, the ability of RARE-seq to simultaneously query for the presence of somatic mutations (for example, EGFRC797S) and the transcriptional effects of pathway activation via somatic alterations (for example, MET amplification) or non-genetic mechanisms (for example, histological transformation) allows more comprehensive non-invasive profiling of resistance mechanisms than previously possible. As histological transformation is a common mechanism of resistance that occurs in diverse cancer types (Ref: 35) and because its diagnosis currently requires invasive tissue biopsies, we envision that its detection using cfRNA will be of broad interest. Future studies focused on each of the potential clinical applications that we explored here will be required to validate our findings and to fully establish the utility of cfRNA analysis in these settings. In addition, it will be interesting to compare and potentially combine RARE-seq with methylation-based and fragmentomics-based liquid biopsy approaches.
Finally, we envision that RARE-seq will be useful in detecting non-cancer-derived cfRNA signatures in patients with both malignant and benign conditions. For example, our data demonstrating the presence of normal lung RNA in the plasma of patients with acute lung injury due to conditions such as COVID-19 infection or acute respiratory distress syndrome suggest that cfRNA analysis may also allow blood-based monitoring of tissue injury. Separately, as demonstrated in the analysis of cfRNA in plasma samples after COVID-19 mRNA vaccination, we envision that RARE-seq will enable simultaneous tracking of mRNA vaccines or RNA therapeutics and measurement of host responses.
In summary, we developed a versatile method for cfRNA analysis that has potential utility in diverse clinical settings. Our results also include several important biological and technical insights that enable more sensitive cfRNA analysis and that will probably be broadly useful for cfRNA-based liquid biopsy applications. We anticipate that our approach could enable novel non-invasive diagnostic applications, with the promise of advancing precision medicine and improving patient care.
